note_4_11_0.js.title: Exam Trap: Orders Are ALWAYS Experimental
note_4_11_0.js.text: You CANNOT deduce the order of reaction from the stoichiometric equation. You must do experiments. Students often make the mistake of assuming order = stoichiometric coefficient (e.g., 2A + B → products is NOT necessarily second order w.r.t. A).

note_4_11_0.js.title: The Factor Test Rule
note_4_11_0.js.text: Memorize: if [concentration] increases by factor x and rate increases by factor x^n, then order = n. Example: triple [A], rate becomes 9 times faster → 9 = 3² → order = 2.

note_4_11_1.js.title: Proving First Order Requires TWO Half-Lives
note_4_11_1.js.text: Many students measure only one half-life and claim it's constant. In an exam, you MUST read two separate half-lives (e.g., A₀→A₀/2 and A₀/2→A₀/4) and explicitly state both are equal to prove first order.

note_4_11_1.js.title: Half-Life Diagnostic Method
note_4_11_1.js.text: Constant t½ = 1st order (ONLY this order has constant t½). Decreasing t½ = 0th order. Increasing t½ = 2nd order. This is the fastest way to identify order from a [Conc] vs Time graph.

note_4_11_2.js.title: Quenching Timing Error
note_4_11_2.js.text: When taking samples, record the time when HALF the aliquot (5 cm³ of 10 cm³) has been transferred into the quenching agent. Students often record the wrong time point, leading to systematic errors in calculated rates.

note_4_11_2.js.title: Purpose of Quenching
note_4_11_2.js.text: Quenching immediately stops the reaction so the concentration does NOT change during titration. Methods: ice bath (dilute + cool), excess standard acid, or remove catalyst. This is essential for accurate rate measurement.

note_4_11_3.js.title: Avoid Curve-Fitting Ambiguity with Rate vs [Conc] Graphs
note_4_11_3.js.text: Plotting Rate directly against Concentration often produces ambiguous curves that could be 2nd, 3rd, or fractional order. Use LOG-LOG GRAPHS instead: plot log(Rate) vs log[Concentration] for a straight line where gradient = order.

note_4_11_3.js.title: Initial Rates Method Logic
note_4_11_3.js.text: Measure the time to produce a small, fixed amount of product (e.g., disappearance of a cross). This eliminates the problem of changing concentrations and gives initial rate directly via Rate ∝ 1/t.

note_4_11_4.js.title: All Other Concentrations Must Stay Constant
note_4_11_4.js.text: When comparing experiments to find order w.r.t. reactant A, the concentrations of ALL OTHER reactants must be identical. If you cannot find such a pair, you must mathematically account for the effect of other concentration changes.

note_4_11_4.js.title: Quick Order Recognition
note_4_11_4.js.text: Double concentration: no rate change = 0th order | rate doubles = 1st order | rate quadruples = 2nd order.

note_4_11_5.js.title: k Units Change With Overall Order
note_4_11_5.js.text: The units of k are NOT fixed. They depend entirely on the overall order. 0th order: mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹ | 1st order: s⁻¹ | 2nd order: dm³ mol⁻¹ s⁻¹ | 3rd order: dm⁶ mol⁻² s⁻¹. Exams always mark this specifically.

note_4_11_5.js.title: Quick Units Check
note_4_11_5.js.text: Rearrange the rate equation to isolate k, then substitute mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹ for Rate and mol dm⁻³ for each [concentration]. Cancel and flip negative indices in denominator to positive in numerator.

note_4_11_6.js.title: Order Tells You RDS Involvement, Not Stoichiometry
note_4_11_6.js.text: Zero order w.r.t. X means X enters AFTER the RDS. First order w.r.t. X means ONE molecule of X is in/before the RDS. Second order w.r.t. X means TWO molecules of X collide in/before the RDS. Do NOT confuse this with stoichiometric coefficients.

note_4_11_6.js.title: Mechanism Consistency Test
note_4_11_6.js.text: To check if a proposed mechanism is consistent with an experimental rate equation: the reactants that appear in the rate equation (and their orders) must all be accounted for in or before the RDS.

note_4_11_7.js.title: Unit Trap: Eₐ is ALWAYS in J mol⁻¹ in Arrhenius Equation
note_4_11_7.js.text: If a question gives Eₐ in kJ mol⁻¹, you MUST convert to J mol⁻¹ by multiplying by 1000 BEFORE using it in k = Ae^(−Eₐ/RT). Using kJ directly will give a nonsensical answer.

note_4_11_7.js.title: Arrhenius Plot: Read Gradient Carefully
note_4_11_7.js.text: Plot ln(k) vs 1/T. The gradient is −Eₐ/R (negative). To find Eₐ: take the absolute value of the gradient and multiply by R (8.31 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹). Then divide by 1000 to convert to kJ mol⁻¹ if needed.

note_4_11_8.js.title: Recording Exact Time in Sampling Experiments
note_4_11_8.js.text: For CP9a, the correct time to record is when HALF of the 10 cm³ aliquot has been transferred (i.e., 5 cm³ has entered the quenching flask). Recording the wrong time point introduces large systematic errors in calculated concentrations.

note_4_11_8.js.title: Iodine Titration Endpoint
note_4_11_8.js.text: Add starch indicator ONLY when the brown iodine solution turns pale yellow (not too early). The endpoint is colourless (when all I₂ has reacted with thiosulfate). This gives the most precise volume reading.

note_4_12_0.js.title: Exothermic ≠ Spontaneous (and Endothermic ≠ Non-Spontaneous)
note_4_12_0.js.text: Ice melting at 25°C is endothermic, yet spontaneous. Freezing at 25°C is exothermic, yet non-spontaneous. ΔH alone CANNOT determine spontaneity. Both entropy and temperature matter via ΔG = ΔH − TΔS.

note_4_12_0.js.title: Kinetic vs Thermodynamic Stability
note_4_12_0.js.text: Petrol + oxygen: thermodynamically very feasible (ΔG << 0), but kinetically stable (no reaction at room temperature without ignition). A reaction can be spontaneous (ΔG < 0) yet appear 'stable' due to a high activation energy barrier.

note_4_12_1.js.title: Entropy Is Quantified by Number of Microstates, Not Vague 'Disorder'
note_4_12_1.js.text: S = k_B ln W is precise. W is the number of possible arrangements. A gas has vastly more possible arrangements than a solid, so higher entropy. Students who think 'disorder' is a physical thing miss the statistical foundation.

note_4_12_1.js.title: More Particles = Higher Entropy
note_4_12_1.js.text: When 1 mole of NaCl(s) dissolves, it produces 2 moles of aqueous ions (Na⁺ + Cl⁻). This doubling of particle count dramatically increases the number of possible microstates, making ΔS_dissolution large and positive.

note_4_12_2.js.title: Boiling Entropy Change Is ~5x Larger Than Melting
note_4_12_2.js.text: For water: ΔS_m ≈ 22 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, but ΔS_b ≈ 109 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. This is because the liquid→gas transition is far more dramatic in terms of particle freedom than solid→liquid. Students often treat entropy changes for all phase transitions as similar.

note_4_12_2.js.title: Predict Entropy Sign Quickly
note_4_12_2.js.text: Melting, boiling, dissolving, gas expansion → ΔS > 0. Freezing, condensation, compression → ΔS < 0. For reactions: count moles of gas. More gas products → ΔS > 0.

note_4_12_3.js.title: ΔS_surroundings = −ΔH_system / T (Negative Sign!)
note_4_12_3.js.text: Common error: writing ΔS_surr = +ΔH_sys / T. The correct sign is NEGATIVE because if the system releases heat (ΔH < 0), the surroundings absorb it (−ΔH > 0), increasing their entropy. Exothermic reactions increase surroundings entropy.

note_4_12_3.js.title: Second Law: ΔS_total > 0 for Spontaneity
note_4_12_3.js.text: A process is spontaneous if ΔS_system + ΔS_surroundings > 0. At constant T and P, this simplifies to ΔG = ΔH − TΔS < 0. Both approaches are equivalent.

note_4_12_4.js.title: ΔG < 0 Means Feasible, NOT Fast
note_4_12_4.js.text: Spontaneity and kinetics are different. Diamond converting to graphite has ΔG << 0 (spontaneous), but Eₐ is so high that it never happens. Coal burns (ΔG << 0) but requires a spark for ignition. ΔG predicts direction, kinetics predicts rate.

note_4_12_4.js.title: Critical Temperature When ΔG = 0
note_4_12_4.js.text: For reactions where ΔH and ΔS have opposite signs, set ΔG = 0 and solve: T_crit = ΔH / ΔS. This is the temperature at which the reaction switches from spontaneous to non-spontaneous (or vice versa).

note_4_12_5.js.title: Charge Effect >>> Radius Effect on Lattice Enthalpy
note_4_12_5.js.text: NaF (singly charged, ~0.233 nm) vs CaF₂ (doubly charged, ~0.236 nm): nearly identical distances, but CaF₂ lattice enthalpy is TRIPLE that of NaF. Charge dominates. Students often overweight the radius effect.

note_4_12_5.js.title: Born-Haber Cycle Setup
note_4_12_5.js.text: Always follow the same sequence: atomisation (ΔH_at) → ionisation (IE) → electron affinity (EA) → lattice formation. The sum equals enthalpy of formation. Use Hess's law: ΔH_form = Σ(ΔH steps) + ΔH_lattice.

note_4_12_6.js.title: Fajans' Rules: Covalent Character When Cation Is Small + Anion Is Large
note_4_12_6.js.text: High charge density of cation polarizes the anion's electrons toward itself, creating covalent overlap. AgI experimental lattice energy is much more negative than theoretical because Ag⁺ heavily polarizes the large I⁻. Experimental ΔH_latt more negative = covalent contribution.

note_4_12_6.js.title: Group 2 Solubility Trends Explained by Hydration vs Lattice Energy
note_4_12_6.js.text: Hydroxides (small anion): lattice energy falls FASTER down group → solubility INCREASES. Sulfates (large anion): hydration falls FASTER → solubility DECREASES. Size of anion determines which enthalpy term dominates.

note_4_12_7.js.title: Most Dissolution Is ENTROPY-Driven Despite Being Endothermic
note_4_12_7.js.text: NH₄Cl dissolves endothermically (ΔH > 0), yet is soluble because ΔS_solution ≈ +70 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ is large. At 298 K, TΔS term dominates. Students incorrectly think exothermic dissolution is more spontaneous.

note_4_12_7.js.title: Most Salts Become MORE Soluble When Heated
note_4_12_7.js.text: For typical salts with ΔH > 0 and ΔS > 0, increasing T makes TΔS grow relative to ΔH, so ΔG becomes more negative → higher solubility. Exceptions like Ca(OH)₂ are rare (exothermic dissolution).

note_4_12_8.js.title: Unit Conversion Trap: ΔS to kJ K⁻¹ mol⁻¹
note_4_12_8.js.text: If ΔS = 100 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, divide by 1000 to get 0.1 kJ K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ before calculating TΔS. Common error: using ΔS = 100 directly → TΔS = 298 × 100 = 29,800 kJ (nonsense). Correct: TΔS = 298 × 0.1 = 29.8 kJ.

note_4_12_8.js.title: ΔG° and Equilibrium Constant K Relationship
note_4_12_8.js.text: ΔG° = −RT ln K. Always convert ΔG° to joules (not kJ) because R = 8.314 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. Large negative ΔG° → large K >> 1 → products favored. ΔG° = 0 → K = 1 → equilibrium.
